characters.relation: Lover-teacher who tried to rein him
characters.blurb: Sought Alcibiades’s soul more than his beauty; saved his life in battle and stood as the one man he truly feared to shame.
Wealthy dove who opposed the Sicilian dream. Ostracism nearly took one of them; Hyperbolus fell instead—and Sicily ruined both.
Kinsman who raised Alcibiades after Clinias’s death—the standard of glory the ward both imitated and outraged.
Died at Coronea; his fame and Alcmaeonid ties left Alcibiades rich, brilliant, and dangerously unsupervised.
Daughter of Hipponicus; tried to leave Alcibiades over his courtesans, then was fetched home from the archon’s office.
Torch-bearer of the Mysteries whose wealth and daughter tied Alcibiades to the first houses of Athens.
Heir of Hipponicus; tangled with Alcibiades in money, marriage, and the gossip of high Athens.

characters.relation: Persian satrap; final host and killer
characters.blurb: Phrygian satrap who alternately helped and hunted him; Lysander finally had him arrange Alcibiades’s murder.
Persian governor in Asia whom Alcibiades dazzled and advised—playing Persia against Sparta for Athens’s sake (and his own).
Navarch who closed the war at Aegospotami and would not leave Alcibiades alive to threaten the new order.
Blunt soldier who wanted to attack Syracuse at once; died early, leaving Nicias alone with a war he hated.
Arrived to stiffen Sicily against Athens; his skill sealed the disaster Alcibiades had sold to the demos.

characters.relation: Orator tangled in the Mysteries scandal
characters.blurb: Named in the Hermae/Mysteries panic that exploded as the Sicilian fleet sailed—the storm that drove Alcibiades to flee.
Tried to ostracize Alcibiades or Nicias; they combined and ostracized him instead—the last such vote.
Third man in the political fight that ended Hyperbolus; polished speaker without Alcibiades’s force.
Host at Sparta whose wife Timaea was said to bear Alcibiades a child—personal hate atop policy.
Queen whispered to have loved Alcibiades; the scandal sharpened Agis’s enmity.
General who opposed recalling Alcibiades; later murdered after playing all sides in the 411 revolution.
Helped bring Alcibiades back to the fleet; later restorer of democracy at Athens.
Shared commands in the Ionian and Hellespontine campaigns of Alcibiades’s second Athenian career.
Fell when Alcibiades and the Athenian captains destroyed his fleet—the peak of the recall years.
Left in charge against orders, he provoked Lysander and lost ships—the excuse Athens used to cashier Alcibiades again.
Courtesan who was with him in Phrygia and wrapped his body after the assassins burned him out.
Commander entangled in the Persian–Spartan deals Alcibiades tried to manage through Tisaphernes.
Appears among the clever, dangerous young men of Alcibiades’s set—foreshadowing the Thirty.
Relative who defended Alcibiades’s interests in the assembly during the recalls and accusations.

Ward of Pericles whose beauty, wealth, and audacity made him the most desired and most dangerous man in Athens. He won great victories, betrayed every city that trusted him, and died by assassins' fire in a Phrygian village—with only Timandra to wrap his body.

characters.relation: Oligarch and rival in the revolution
characters.blurb: Played all sides in the 411 coup and its collapse; his moves against Alcibiades and Phrynichus illustrate the lethal politics of the age of the Thirty.
Wealthy tanner-politician who suffered Alcibiades's contemptuous treatment in youth—Plutarch tells the story of wine thrown in his face, which Anytus's admirers found charming rather than insulting.

The famous Athenian misanthrope who despised all mankind except Alcibiades—because, he said, the young man would one day cause great calamity to Athens. Plutarch records the prophecy with evident irony.
Popular leader who stoked the Mysteries panic to drive Alcibiades from the city before the Sicilian fleet sailed; he was himself later killed in the political violence that followed.
Pericles's brother who shared legal guardianship of Alcibiades and Clinias after their father's death—but it was Pericles's household that shaped the boy.

characters.relation: Philosopher who wrote him into the dialogues
characters.blurb: Wrote the Symposium and the two Alcibiades dialogues; his portrait of the brilliant, vain, and beloved young man is the deepest literary account Plutarch cites.

Judged Alcibiades's conduct of the war brilliantly but his private life disastrously; his cool assessment of the Sicilian expedition as genius undone by personal scandal stands behind Plutarch's account.
In the Frogs and elsewhere Aristophanes caught Athens's ambivalence about Alcibiades—that the city both longed for him and feared him, missing him as it damned him.
Brilliant speechwriter involved in the Four Hundred's revolution; named among the clever, dangerous figures whose careers intersect Alcibiades's last Athenian years.
An Athenian who gave Alcibiades money to buy a horse at Olympia; Alcibiades entered it under his own name instead—a piece of shameless appropriation Plutarch records without softening.
The Hermae—herm-pillars of Hermes—were mutilated on the eve of the Sicilian fleet's departure; the sacrilege was blamed on Alcibiades's circle and ignited the Mysteries panic.
The Eleusinian Mysteries of Demeter and Persephone were said to have been parodied at Alcibiades's private parties—the charge that made him a target for prosecution even as the fleet sailed.

characters.relation: Priestess who refused to curse him
characters.blurb: When the assembly ordered Athenian priests to curse Alcibiades formally, Theano alone refused, saying she was a priestess to pray, not to curse—a moment of quiet dignity Plutarch admires.

characters.relation: Wealthy host of the Mysteries parody
characters.blurb: Named alongside others at whose houses the sacred Eleusinian rites were reportedly mocked—the social network of the scandal that drove Alcibiades to defect to Sparta.

characters.relation: Poet Alcibiades sought from a schoolmaster
characters.blurb: When a teacher said he had no Homer, Alcibiades struck him—Plutarch uses the story to show the young man's ferocious expectations of learning.

characters.relation: Tragedian cited for the Sicilian glory
characters.blurb: Euripides's account of the Sicilian spectacle is invoked by Plutarch alongside Thucydides when describing the splendor of the expedition Alcibiades championed.

characters.relation: Herald in the Mysteries parody; later refused to curse
characters.blurb: Played herald in the private Mysteries parody; afterward, when the priesthood was ordered to curse Alcibiades, Theodorus of Phegaea alone refused, saying he was a priest to pray, not to curse.

locations.relation: Home city; political base
locations.blurb: Ward of Pericles; came of age in the assembly and the agora. Every defection and recall began and ended at Athens, which loved, feared, and finally abandoned him.

locations.relation: First campaign; shared a tent with Socrates
locations.blurb: Alcibiades and Socrates served side by side here; Socrates saved the wounded Alcibiades and later yielded the prize of valor to him at the army's awards.

locations.relation: Second campaign; Socrates covered the retreat
locations.blurb: At the rout of Delium, Alcibiades on horseback escorted the retreating Socrates on foot—Socrates calm amid the chaos, Alcibiades covering him.

locations.relation: First landing on the Sicilian expedition
locations.blurb: First touch of Italian soil on the great expedition; Alcibiades argued for an immediate advance on Syracuse while Nicias urged caution—the disagreement that would prove fatal.

locations.relation: Athens's only Sicilian foothold
locations.blurb: All that was done while Alcibiades commanded in the west: he sailed for Sicily and took Catana before the recall order came and the disaster began without him.

locations.relation: First stop on his flight from the recall
locations.blurb: He went ashore at Thurii and hid from his Athenian escort, beginning the famous flight that would take him to Argos, then to Sparta itself.

locations.relation: Refuge in exile; helped fortify the city
locations.blurb: First refuge in Peloponnese; he helped Argos build long walls to the sea for the Athenian alliance, and later returned here briefly after fleeing the Mysteries charges.

locations.relation: Enemy city he adopted—and undermined
locations.blurb: Conformed so perfectly to Spartan ways—bathing cold, eating black broth—that people marveled. He advised the fortification of Decelea in Attica and stirred the Ionian revolts, while Agis's wife was rumored to love him.

locations.relation: First Ionian revolt he sparked for Sparta
locations.blurb: Sailed with the Spartan Chalcideus to stir Chios into revolt after the Sicilian disaster; the first of Athens's eastern allies he helped tear away.

locations.relation: Athenian fleet's base; scene of recall
locations.blurb: The democratic sailors here recalled Alcibiades and gave him command of the Aegean campaign. From Samos he reversed Athens's fortunes, then returned here a final time before Lysander's victory ended everything.

locations.relation: Prisoned here by Tisaphernes; escaped
locations.blurb: Tisaphernes arrested him and sent him here as prisoner to clear himself of Persian suspicion; Alcibiades escaped after thirty days by horse to Clazomenae.

locations.relation: Greatest Athenian victory of the late war
locations.blurb: Lured the full Peloponnesian fleet, ran it ashore, killed Mindarus, and seized Cyzicus—with forty ships feigning weakness until the rest closed the trap. The Hellespont was Athens's again.

locations.relation: Siege and battle on the Bosphorus
locations.blurb: Walled off from sea to sea; defeated Pharnabazus and the Spartan governor Hippocrates in the same action. The siege ended in a treaty useful to Athens.

locations.relation: City taken by audacity with thirty men
locations.blurb: Entering by a premature signal with only thirty men, he bluffed the armed city into parley, then preserved it from looting—characteristic mixture of recklessness and magnanimity.

locations.relation: Taken by night; secured the grain route
locations.blurb: Feigned departure, slipped back by night, entered the walls while the fleet attacked the harbor loudly. The city fell; Athens's vital Pontic grain route was secured.

locations.relation: Visited during final command; neighbor of Notium disaster
locations.blurb: He had gone to Ephesus when his pilot Antiochus disobeyed orders and lost ships to Lysander nearby—the disaster that gave Athens's enemies the excuse to strip Alcibiades of his command.

locations.relation: Independent base after removal from command
locations.blurb: Raised mercenaries and made his own war against the free Thracians, building a stronghold and amassing treasure after Athens cashiered him a second time.

locations.relation: Warned the Athenian generals; dismissed
locations.blurb: Rode down from his Thracian stronghold to warn the Athenian generals their anchorage was exposed. They dismissed him. Lysander destroyed the fleet the next morning.

locations.relation: Refuge after Lysander's victory
locations.blurb: Fled here from the Spartan-dominated world after Aegospotami; lost much of his treasure to the Bithynians before moving inland to Phrygia and Pharnabazus's province.

locations.relation: Site of his death by fire and arrows
locations.blurb: Died in a small Phrygian village: Pharnabazus's men surrounded the house and set it ablaze; Alcibiades burst through the fire with a sword but was shot down by their darts. Timandra wrapped his body.

locations.relation: The expedition he championed and was recalled from
locations.blurb: The dream he sold to Athens—conquest of the west. He took Catana and was poised to move on Syracuse when the Mysteries scandal called him home; the expedition failed utterly without him.

locations.relation: Region where he wore each country's character
locations.blurb: In Ionia he was said to be luxurious and gay—the most striking example of his chameleon gift. He stirred the Ionian cities to revolt against Athens while working for both Sparta and Tisaphernes.

locations.relation: Strategic strait he secured for Athens
locations.blurb: The prize of the later war: control of the straits meant control of Athens's grain supply. Cyzicus and Chalcedon secured it; Aegospotami lost it forever.

locations.relation: Battle where his timely arrival turned the tide
locations.blurb: He arrived with eighteen ships at Abydos at the crisis of a fleet battle; his sudden appearance rallied the Athenians and drove the Peloponnesians ashore, uniting two broken forces.

locations.relation: Island that revolted after the Sicilian disaster
locations.blurb: Among the allies that signaled revolt after Sicily; the Spartan expedition Alcibiades helped direct aimed for Chios before Lesbos, rippling Athenian power apart from east to west.
